title: Large Language Models for Depression Screening: A Systematic Evaluation Across Clinical Text Corpora
venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems
abstract: We present a systematic evaluation of large language models (LLMs) for automated depression screening using clinical interview transcripts, social media text, and electronic health records. Our findings show that fine-tuned LLMs achieve performance competitive with trained clinicians on standardised depression scales, while also surfacing important fairness considerations across demographic subgroups.

title: Predicting Psychological Distress from Financial Behaviour Patterns: A Machine Learning Approach
abstract: Using anonymised transaction-level banking data linked to mental health survey outcomes, we train gradient-boosted classifiers to predict self-reported psychological distress up to three months in advance. Our model achieves AUROC of 0.81 and highlights spending volatility and income irregularity as leading predictors of deteriorating mental health.

title: Passive Sensing and Ecological Momentary Assessment for Mood Monitoring: A Scoping Review
abstract: We review 87 studies using smartphone passive sensing and ecological momentary assessment (EMA) for mood monitoring in clinical and non-clinical populations, synthesising findings on accuracy, user burden, and ethical considerations.

title: Algorithmic Fairness in Mental Health AI: Challenges and Opportunities
venue: ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability and Transparency
abstract: We critically examine fairness gaps in deployed mental health AI tools, demonstrating disparate performance across gender, ethnicity, and socioeconomic strata. We propose a fairness-aware evaluation protocol and release benchmark datasets to support reproducible research.
